name: Portia
title: Arbiter
note: This draft has a fatal structural failure: **the chapter opens with immediate dramatic stakes ("trapped in the cave by a grizzly") but then flashbacks to recount everything that led there, destroying all the tension it just promised.** You telegraph the endpoint in your opening line ("Nora returned to the basin three days after meeting Aris, alone this time"), which tells us she survives whatever happens, then spend the entire chapter walking us through discoveries (bones, tools, petroglyphs, hum) we now experience as mere backstory to reach a cliffhanger we already know is coming. Either open *in medias res* with her already trapped and reveal the discoveries through her desperate mental inventory, or build chronologically so each discovery escalates genuine tension toward an *unknown* climax.

name: Horatio
title: Realist
note: The description of "the Handler's impossible prints" asks the reader to accept their 'impossibility' without immediate, sensory justification in this scene. While Nora identifies them, the text tells us *that* they are impossible rather than showing us *why*—a missed chance to ground that claim in a concrete detail that would solidify its unsettling nature for a skeptical eye. Without a detail like unusual size or number of digits, the reader must simply trust the assertion.

name: Quixote
title: Visionary
note: The draft misses a grand opportunity to explore the surreal or philosophical implications of the Handler's influence over the bears. When Nora traces the petroglyphs and realizes the Handler is not just controlling but teaching the bears, the text briefly flirts with a profound transformation in the natural order but retreats before truly exploring the ramifications. Inviting readers to sit with that strange tension between nature and manipulated evolution could intensify Nora's dawning comprehension. Instead of moving quickly to the hum, pause here: what does this revelation about the bears mean for the essence of wildness, the autonomy of animal life? Is the Handler elevating, corrupting, or revealing something fundamental about the bears—or even the mountain itself? Let the reader dwell in that awe and unease a bit longer before the action resumes.
